[TopazCreek 2026-04-09] From the Phase 1 flamegraph evidence:

BTreeMap::insert = 2.93% of CPU and __memcmp_avx2_movbe = 12.73% of CPU. Combined ~15% is structural BTreeMap overhead. This is the SINGLE LARGEST remaining optimization opportunity.

The entries BTreeMap is at crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:772. Key concern: SCAN cursor implementation. Current SCAN likely relies on BTreeMap's sorted iteration. A HashMap migration requires re-implementing SCAN with epoch-based or snapshot-based cursor.

The state_digest function also iterates entries in sorted order for its hash chain. After this migration, state_digest MUST switch to XOR-of-per-entry-hashes (order-independent). This is also a prerequisite for frankenredis-n5xv (incremental digest), so it's a twofer.

title: Audit SCAN/KEYS/SORT ordering assumptions before BTreeMap→HashMap migration
description:
# Audit Ordering Assumptions

## What

Before replacing BTreeMap with HashMap in Store::entries, systematically identify
every code path that depends on BTreeMap's sorted iteration order. These must be
adapted for HashMap's non-deterministic iteration.

## Specific Areas to Audit

### 1. SCAN / HSCAN / SSCAN / ZSCAN
- Location: crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs — search for scan-related functions
- Current behavior: likely iterates BTreeMap in sorted order with a cursor
  that's a key-position offset
- Redis behavior: uses reversed-bit-increment cursor over hash bucket indices;
  no ordering guarantee; may return duplicates; guarantees full coverage of
  keys that exist throughout the entire scan
- Action needed: re-implement cursor as either epoch-based (last-seen-key) or
  snapshot-based

### 2. KEYS pattern matching
- Location: crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs — search for keys_matching or similar
- Current behavior: returns keys in BTreeMap sorted order
- Redis behavior: no ordering guarantee
- Action needed: verify no conformance tests depend on sorted KEYS output;
  if they do, mark as over-specified and fix tests

### 3. SORT command with BY nosort
- Location: crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs — SORT handler
- Current behavior: NOSORT returns elements in "natural order"
- Redis behavior: for lists, NOSORT returns insertion order (list-internal);
  for sets, returns arbitrary order; doesn't depend on entries map order
- Action needed: verify SORT doesn't use entries iteration order

### 4. state_digest computation
- Location: crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:7875
- Current behavior: iterates entries in sorted order, chains FNV hashes
- Action needed: MUST change to order-independent algorithm (XOR-of-per-entry-
  hashes) regardless. This is also a prerequisite for frankenredis-n5xv.
- Note: state_digest is now on the cold path (lazy-digest refactor), so the
  algorithm change is safe; the only concern is digest stability for conformance
  fixtures that check evidence-ledger digests. Search for hard-coded digest
  values in golden_outputs/ and conformance fixtures.

### 5. DEBUG / OBJECT / internal dump paths
- Any path that iterates all keys and depends on order

### 6. Conformance fixtures
- Search golden_outputs/ for any fixture that asserts key ordering
- Check if any conformance test sorts keys differently or depends on
  deterministic iteration

## Deliverable

A markdown checklist of every ordering-dependent path, with the required
adaptation strategy for each. This checklist becomes the implementation
guide for the migration subtask.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Complete audit document covering all 6 areas above
2. No unidentified ordering dependency in the codebase
3. Each dependency has a named adaptation strategy
close_reason: Audit completed; ordering dependencies documented and implementation strategy chosen

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: [CalmOak 2026-04-09] Audit summary: deterministic ordering dependencies are confirmed in runtime KEYS/SCAN, store active-expire cursoring, state_digest, RDB snapshot export via all_keys(), and unit/conformance tests that hard-code sorted KEYS/SCAN output. Implementation path: HashMap for Store::entries plus an auxiliary ordered key index to preserve deterministic cold-path behavior while moving hot-path lookups off BTreeMap.

title: Implement HashMap migration for Store::entries
description:
# Implement HashMap Migration

## What

Replace BTreeMap<Vec<u8>, Entry> with HashMap<Vec<u8>, Entry> at
crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:772 and adapt all ordering-dependent code paths
identified in the audit subtask (frankenredis-hs04.1).

## Key Implementation Steps

### 1. Change the type declaration
```rust
// Before:
entries: BTreeMap<Vec<u8>, Entry>,

// After:
entries: HashMap<Vec<u8>, Entry>,
```

### 2. Fix SCAN cursor
Implement epoch-based cursor strategy:
- SCAN 0 starts a new iteration, returns a batch of N keys + next cursor
- Cursor encodes the iteration state (e.g., hash of last-seen key + count)
- On SCAN <cursor>, find position and return next batch
- Guarantee: all keys present throughout full scan are returned (may duplicate)

### 3. Fix state_digest
Change from sequential FNV chain to XOR-of-per-entry-hashes:
```rust
pub fn state_digest(&self) -> String {
    let mut hash = 0_u64;
    for (key, entry) in &self.entries {
        hash ^= entry_hash(key, entry);
    }
    format!("{hash:016x}")
}
```

### 4. Fix any test assertions
Update tests that depend on sorted key iteration order.

### 5. Consider HashMap capacity hints
Pre-size the HashMap based on expected keyspace. The default load factor
(7/8 for std HashMap) is fine; don't over-optimize.

## Files to Change

- crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs — primary change (entries type + all methods)
- crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs — if any iteration depends on order
- golden_outputs/ — if any fixtures have order-dependent expected output

## This Must Come After

The audit subtask (frankenredis-hs04.1) must complete first to identify all
adaptation points. Implementing without the audit risks silent ordering bugs
that only surface in production under specific key distributions.
close_reason: Implemented and validated

title: Benchmark + conformance gate for HashMap migration
description:
# Benchmark + Conformance Gate for HashMap Migration

## What

After frankenredis-hs04.2 (HashMap implementation) is complete, run the full
quality gate and produce a before/after delta artifact.

## Steps

1. Build release binary with HashMap changes
2. Run fr-bench for all workloads: set, get, incr, mixed (p1 and p16)
3. Capture perf flamegraph to confirm BTreeMap::insert and memcmp overhead
   is reduced
4. Run cargo test --workspace (all unit tests)
5. Run cargo test -p fr-conformance (all 43 conformance tests)
6. Verify SCAN works correctly with a multi-cursor full-scan test
7. Compare throughput numbers to the April 9 baseline
8. Write isomorphism proof artifact at
   artifacts/optimization/hashmap-migration/ISOMORPHISM_PROOF.md

## Expected Outcome

BTreeMap::insert (2.93%) and __memcmp_avx2_movbe (12.73%) should both drop
significantly. Combined ~15% CPU savings → estimated 10-15% throughput
improvement. SET p1 should move from 75K to ~85K+ ops/sec.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. All 43 conformance tests pass
2. All workspace tests pass
3. Benchmark shows measurable improvement (>5% on SET)
4. Flamegraph confirms BTreeMap is no longer in top 10 CPU consumers
5. Isomorphism proof artifact is written and checked in
6. README updated with new numbers if improvement is significant

title: EPIC: Establish real performance baseline with redis-benchmark-compatible harness and p50/p95/p99 latency tracking
description:
# Performance Evidence Epic

## Why This Is Critical
The README describes "Deterministic Latency Replication Core (DLRC)" as the "Crown-Jewel Innovation" and claims "profile-proven performance" with "p95/p99 latency tracking." Yet the repo contains ZERO throughput benchmarks, ZERO latency percentile measurements, and an empty baselines/ directory. The only checked-in artifacts measure conformance test execution speed (~1.3ms), not Redis workload performance.

This is the single most dangerous gap because performance is the stated reason this project exists as a differentiator from legacy Redis. Without evidence, DLRC is marketing copy, not engineering.

## What Success Looks Like
- A redis-benchmark-compatible harness that measures ops/sec, latency histograms (p50/p95/p99/p999), and memory usage under mixed workloads
- Baseline numbers checked into baselines/ with comparison against legacy Redis on identical workloads
- CI-gatable regression detection: any commit that degrades p99 by >10% fails the gate
- The README performance claims are either proven or honestly revised

## Architecture
The benchmark harness should be a new binary in fr-server or a dedicated crate that:
1. Connects via real TCP to a running frankenredis instance
2. Runs configurable workloads (SET/GET mixed, pipeline depth, key sizes)
3. Records latency histograms using HdrHistogram or equivalent
4. Outputs JSON artifacts compatible with the existing artifacts/ structure
5. Compares against baselines/ and reports deltas

## Dependencies
All sub-tasks feed into this epic. The benchmark harness must exist before any optimization work can be proven.

title: Implement replication backlog buffer that stores command bytes for partial resync
description:
# Replication Backlog Buffer

## What
The existing `BacklogWindow` in fr-repl tracks replication offsets (replid, start offset, size) but does NOT store actual command bytes. For partial resync (PSYNC CONTINUE) to work, the primary must buffer recent write commands so that a reconnecting replica can receive just the delta instead of a full resync.

## Current State
- `BacklogWindow` struct exists with replid, start_offset, size fields
- `decide_psync()` checks if requested offset falls within the backlog window
- But no actual byte buffer exists — when CONTINUE is decided, there are no bytes to send

## Implementation
1. Add a `backlog_bytes: VecDeque<u8>` field to the replication state (either in ServerState or a new BacklogBuffer struct)
2. Configure max size via `repl-backlog-size` (already a config knob, default 1 MiB)
3. After each write command is propagated, append its RESP-serialized bytes to the backlog
4. When backlog exceeds max size, drain from the front (oldest bytes)
5. Track the byte offset corresponding to the front of the buffer
6. On PSYNC CONTINUE: calculate the byte range the replica needs, send those bytes
7. On PSYNC FULLRESYNC: reset backlog after snapshot send

## Unit Tests
- Test 1: Append 100 commands, verify backlog size stays within limit
- Test 2: Partial resync — append commands, request offset in range, verify correct bytes returned
- Test 3: Offset out of range — verify FULLRESYNC fallback
- Test 4: Backlog wraps — oldest bytes evicted when limit reached
- Test 5: Reset on FULLRESYNC — verify backlog clears

## Depends On
- REPLICAOF TCP client (1v8b) — need connection before backlog matters
- Must be done BEFORE command propagation (1ng7) — propagation writes to this buffer

## Files
- crates/fr-repl/src/lib.rs (backlog buffer struct + methods)
- crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs (wire backlog into server state)
close_reason: Already implemented: aof_records Vec<AofRecord> in ServerState stores command bytes, encoded_aof_stream_from_offset() extracts delta for partial resync, PSYNC CONTINUE handler at main.rs:1642-1644 sends backlog bytes to reconnecting replicas.

title: Add expired/evicted key counters to Store
description:
# Add expired_keys and evicted_keys counters to Store

## What
Add four new pub u64 fields to Store:
- stat_expired_keys: total keys removed by expiration (lazy or active)
- stat_evicted_keys: total keys removed by maxmemory eviction
- stat_expired_stale_perc: percentage of expired keys found during sampling (for active-expire efficiency)
- stat_expire_cycle_cpu_milliseconds: cumulative CPU time spent in active-expire cycles

## Where to increment
- stat_expired_keys: in drop_if_expired() (lazy expiry) and in the active-expire cycle in fr-runtime
- stat_evicted_keys: in the eviction cycle in fr-runtime (handle_eviction)
- The fr-runtime eviction and active-expire code already has the logic — it just doesn't count

## Key implementation points
- Add fields to Store struct (fr-store/src/lib.rs near line 722)
- Initialize to 0 in Store::new()
- For lazy expiry: increment stat_expired_keys inside drop_if_expired() when a key is actually removed
- For active expiry: the runtime's active_expire_cycle already iterates and removes keys — add counting there
- For eviction: the runtime's eviction_cycle already removes keys — add counting there
- Reset in CONFIG RESETSTAT (already wired in runtime)

## Why this matters
- INFO stats reports expired_keys:0 and evicted_keys:0 — essential for monitoring memory pressure
- total_keys_expired and total_keys_evicted are aliases of the same counters
- Redis monitoring tools alert on eviction rate increases

## Testing
- Unit test: set key with TTL, advance time past expiry, verify stat_expired_keys incremented
- Conformance: INFO stats after expiry operations should show non-zero counts

## Files to modify
- crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs (Store struct + drop_if_expired)
- crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs (active_expire_cycle, eviction_cycle)

title: Fix NOMKSTREAM behavior for existing empty streams
description:
## Problem

fr-command/src/lib.rs lines 4000-4007: XADD with NOMKSTREAM incorrectly returns nil for existing empty streams (created via XGROUP CREATE ... MKSTREAM).

The NOMKSTREAM check uses xlen==0 AND xlast_id==None to determine the stream doesn't exist. But an empty stream (created by MKSTREAM) has xlen==0 and may have xlast_id==None, causing NOMKSTREAM to return nil when it should proceed.

## What Redis Does

NOMKSTREAM only prevents creating a NEW stream key. If the key already exists as a stream (even empty), XADD proceeds normally.

## Fix

Check whether the key exists as a stream type, not whether the stream has entries:
  if nomkstream && !store.key_exists_as_type(key, \"stream\") {
      return Ok(RespFrame::BulkString(None));
  }

This requires either a new store method or checking the entry type directly.

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs: XADD NOMKSTREAM check
- crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs: May need a type-checking helper
close_reason: Fixed: NOMKSTREAM now uses store.exists() to check key existence, not xlen/xlast_id

title: Centralize version string as single const across all crates
description:
## Problem

The Redis version string appears in multiple locations:
- fr-command/src/lib.rs: \"redis_version:7.2.0-frankenredis\" (INFO)
- fr-runtime/src/lib.rs: hello_bulk(\"7.2.0\") (HELLO response)
- fr-runtime/src/lib.rs: (\"redis-ver\", \"7.2.0\") (RDB aux field — was 7.0.0, fixed to 7.2.0 in f8c5a8a)

While these are now consistent (all 7.2.0), there's no single source of truth. If the version needs to change, all three locations must be found and updated.

## Fix

1. Define a const in fr-runtime (or a shared crate): pub const REDIS_COMPAT_VERSION: &str = \"7.2.0\";
2. Use this const in all locations
3. Consider also deriving the FrankenRedis version from Cargo.toml via env!(\"CARGO_PKG_VERSION\")

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs: Define const, use in RDB and HELLO
- crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs: Use const in INFO

title: [EPIC] Dynamic Server Configuration — Wire CONFIG SET to Runtime Enforcement
description:
## Overview

FrankenRedis has numerous hardcoded constants that Redis makes configurable via CONFIG SET/GET. While the config table in fr-runtime advertises these parameters, the actual enforcement code uses compile-time constants, creating a correctness gap where CONFIG SET appears to succeed but has no effect on server behavior.

## Why This Matters

1. **Observability**: Clients that read CONFIG GET or INFO to verify configuration see stale/wrong values
2. **Drop-in parity**: Redis operators expect CONFIG SET maxclients/hz/timeout to take effect immediately
3. **Security**: The bind address defaulting to 0.0.0.0 while config says 127.0.0.1 is a security hole
4. **Testing**: Integration tests that change config at runtime get wrong behavior

## Scope

This epic covers wiring the following runtime-configurable parameters from CONFIG SET through to their actual enforcement points:
- maxclients (server connection limit)
- hz (event loop frequency)
- client-query-buffer-limit / proto-max-bulk-len
- client-output-buffer-limit
- repl-backlog-size
- repl-timeout / repl-ping-replica-period
- busy-reply-threshold (lua-time-limit)
- maxmemory-samples
- bind address

## Architecture Note

The challenge is that CONFIG SET runs in fr-runtime, but enforcement happens in fr-server (connection acceptance, buffer limits, timeouts). We need a communication channel — either shared state on ServerState that fr-server reads each tick, or a config-change event queue.

## Evidence of the problem

INFO hz/configured_hz was hardcoded to 10 (fixed in commit f8c5a8a). INFO maxclients was hardcoded to 10000 (fixed). But the actual enforcement in fr-server still uses compile-time MAX_CLIENTS=10000, QUERY_BUFFER_LIMIT=1MB, etc. CONFIG SET changes these in the config table but the server never reads the updated values.
close_reason: Epic acknowledged — children tasks now unblocked for implementation

title: Forward BGREWRITEAOF from dispatch_argv to runtime via Store flag

# Forward BGREWRITEAOF from dispatch_argv to runtime via Store flag

## Problem
BGREWRITEAOF in fr-command returns the success message without actually writing. The runtime has the real implementation. Lua redis.call(\"BGREWRITEAOF\") is a no-op.

## Design
Add a pub bgrewriteaof_requested: bool flag to Store. When fr-command's bgrewriteaof_cmd is called, set this flag to true and return the success message. The runtime checks this flag after each dispatch and performs the actual AOF rewrite.

## Implementation
1. Add pub bgrewriteaof_requested: bool to Store (default false)
2. fr-command bgrewriteaof_cmd sets store.bgrewriteaof_requested = true
3. Runtime checks store.bgrewriteaof_requested after execute_frame and calls persist_snapshot_to_disk
4. Clear the flag after handling

## Why this pattern
Same as DEBUG RELOAD — the fr-command layer can signal intent, the runtime fulfills it. This is the cleanest cross-layer communication without restructuring dispatch_argv's signature.

## Testing
- Integration test: verify AOF file is written when BGREWRITEAOF called from Lua
- Conformance: existing BGREWRITEAOF test should still pass

## Files to modify
- crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs (flag field)
- crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs (set flag)
- crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs (check flag, perform rewrite)

title: Implement ACL SAVE/LOAD file persistence
description:
# Implement ACL SAVE/LOAD file persistence

## What
ACL SAVE and ACL LOAD currently return OK without persisting. They should:
- ACL SAVE: serialize all ACL user rules to the ACL file (path from config)
- ACL LOAD: read and apply ACL rules from the ACL file, replacing current rules

## Design
Redis stores ACL rules in a text file, one user per line, in the same format as ACL SETUSER:
```
user default on ~* &* +@all
user admin on >password ~* &* +@all
user readonly on >pass ~* &* +@read -@write
```

## Implementation plan
1. Add pub acl_file_path: Option<PathBuf> to Runtime's ServerState (configured via CONFIG SET aclfile)
2. Add fn serialize_acl_rules(&self) -> String to AuthState that outputs one line per user
3. Add fn load_acl_rules(&mut self, content: &str) -> Result<(), String> to AuthState that parses lines
4. ACL SAVE: write serialize_acl_rules() to acl_file_path atomically (temp file + rename)
5. ACL LOAD: read acl_file_path, call load_acl_rules(), return OK or error
6. If acl_file_path is None, return "ERR There is no configured ACL file" (matches Redis)

## Serialization format
Each user line: `user <name> <on|off> [>password...] [~pattern...] [&pattern...] [+command|-command|+@cat|-@cat|allcommands|nocommands|allkeys|allchannels|reset]`

## Key details
- ACL LOAD replaces ALL users except "default" (which is always reset first then reconfigured)
- If parsing fails on any line, ACL LOAD rolls back to previous state and returns error
- Atomic file writes: write to .tmp then rename (already established pattern in fr-persist)

## Testing
- Unit test: serialize → load round-trip preserves all user rules
- Unit test: ACL LOAD with invalid content returns error and preserves previous state
- Conformance: ACL SAVE + ACL LOAD round-trip test

## Files to modify
- crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs (handle_acl_command, AuthState methods)
- crates/fr-config/src/lib.rs (if acl_file_path config is needed)

title: Implement SHUTDOWN graceful server termination
description:
## Problem

fr-command/src/lib.rs line 10729: SHUTDOWN command returns OK but does not actually shut down the server. The comment says: \"Stub -- in production this would trigger graceful shutdown\".

## What Redis Does

SHUTDOWN [NOSAVE | SAVE] [NOW] [FORCE] [ABORT]:
1. Optionally triggers a final SAVE (RDB snapshot)
2. Flushes AOF if configured
3. Closes all client connections gracefully
4. Exits the process

SHUTDOWN ABORT cancels a pending shutdown.
SHUTDOWN NOSAVE skips the final snapshot.

## Implementation Plan

Since SHUTDOWN is a runtime-special command, it needs to signal the server event loop to begin graceful termination:

1. Add a shutdown_requested flag to ServerState
2. SHUTDOWN handler sets the flag (and optionally triggers SAVE)
3. fr-server main loop checks the flag each tick
4. On shutdown: flush writes, close connections, drop listener, exit

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs: ServerState flag, SHUTDOWN handler logic
- crates/fr-server/src/main.rs: Check flag in main loop, graceful exit
close_reason: SHUTDOWN: sets shutdown_requested flag, server event loop exits gracefully with optional final SAVE

title: Implement incremental store digest (O(1) per command)
description:
# Implement Incremental Store Digest (O(1) per command)

## What

Store::state_digest() (crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:7875-7928) walks every entry
in the database, hashing keys, values (including per-element for hashes/lists/
sets/zsets/streams), and expiry times. This is O(N_keys * avg_value_size).

After the April 9 lazy-digest refactor (frankenredis-zjii), this function is no
longer called on the hot path — it's deferred to inside record_threat_event(),
which only fires when emit_evidence_ledger=true AND a threat event occurs (parse
error, auth denial, slow command, etc.). The success path pays zero cost.

However, when a threat DOES fire, the full O(N) scan still happens. At 1M keys,
this can take 10-100ms, blocking the event loop during a security-critical event
recording. This is exactly when you want the server to respond quickly.

## Why

Replace the full-scan implementation with a running digest that's maintained
incrementally on every store mutation. Each insert/update/delete XORs out the
old entry's hash and XORs in the new one. The digest is always O(1) to read.

## Design

Add a `digest: u64` field to Store. On every mutation:

```rust
fn update_digest_for_entry(&mut self, key: &[u8], old_entry: Option<&Entry>, new_entry: Option<&Entry>) {
    if let Some(old) = old_entry {
        self.digest ^= entry_hash(key, old);
    }
    if let Some(new) = new_entry {
        self.digest ^= entry_hash(key, new);
    }
}
```

Where entry_hash computes FNV-1a over key + type_tag + value_bytes + expiry.
XOR is commutative and associative, so order doesn't matter. The running
digest matches what a full scan would produce (with the XOR-based algorithm
rather than the current sequential FNV chain).

## Mutation Points to Instrument

Every path that modifies Store::entries needs to call update_digest_for_entry:
- insert (set, hset, lpush, sadd, zadd, xadd, etc.)
- update (append, incr, setrange, etc.)
- delete (del, expire eviction, pop, etc.)
- bulk operations (flushdb, rename, copy)
- expiry changes (expire, pexpire, persist, etc.)

This is a broad surface area. The key correctness invariant:
self.digest == self.state_digest_full_scan() at all times.
Add a debug assertion that checks this periodically (e.g., every 1000 mutations
in debug builds).

## Dependency on BTreeMap→HashMap Migration

If frankenredis-hs04 (BTreeMap→HashMap) lands first and changes state_digest to
use XOR-of-entry-hashes (order-independent), this bead becomes much simpler —
just maintain the XOR accumulator incrementally rather than recomputing it.

If this bead lands first, state_digest must be changed to XOR-based anyway for
incrementality, which is a prerequisite for the HashMap migration's digest
compatibility. So either ordering works, but hs04-first is cleaner.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Store has a `running_digest: u64` field updated on every mutation
2. state_digest() returns the running value in O(1)
3. Debug assertions verify digest consistency every N mutations
4. All 43 conformance tests pass
5. Threat events still record correct digests
6. Benchmark shows no measurable overhead from incremental tracking
close_reason: Already implemented: running_digest field exists in Store, update_digest_hashes() maintains it incrementally via XOR, state_digest() returns O(1) when digest_stale is false, debug assertions verify correctness

title: Move SLOWLOG state to Store so dispatch_argv path returns real data
description:
# Move SLOWLOG state to Store for Lua/dispatch_argv compatibility

## Problem
SLOWLOG GET/LEN/RESET in fr-command (the dispatch_argv path) returns empty arrays/0/OK because the slow log lives in Runtime's ServerState. Lua scripts calling redis.call(\"SLOWLOG\", \"GET\") get nothing.

## Design
Move the slowlog data structure from ServerState to Store:
- pub slowlog: VecDeque<SlowlogEntry>
- pub slowlog_id_counter: u64
- pub slowlog_log_slower_than_us: i64
- pub slowlog_max_len: usize

## Implementation
1. Define SlowlogEntry in fr-store (or move from fr-runtime):
   ```rust
   pub struct SlowlogEntry {
       pub id: u64,
       pub timestamp_sec: u64,
       pub duration_us: u64,
       pub argv: Vec<Vec<u8>>,
   }
   ```
2. Add the fields to Store
3. Add Store methods: record_slowlog(), get_slowlog(), slowlog_len(), reset_slowlog()
4. Update fr-command's slowlog_cmd to accept &mut Store and call these methods
5. Update fr-runtime to call store.record_slowlog() instead of server.record_slowlog()
6. Remove duplicate SlowlogEntry from runtime (or re-export from store)

## Why Store instead of Runtime
- Store is accessible from both dispatch_argv (Lua scripts, AOF replay) and the runtime
- The slow log is essentially per-database state, making Store the natural home
- This follows the same pattern we used for CONFIG GET/SET

## Migration path
- The runtime already has record_slowlog wired into execute_frame — just redirect to store
- slowlog_log_slower_than_us and slowlog_max_len are already CONFIG SET-able in runtime — redirect to store fields

## Testing
- Conformance: Lua script calls redis.call(\"SLOWLOG\", \"GET\") and gets real entries
- Unit test: record a slow command, verify SLOWLOG LEN returns 1

## Files to modify
- crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs (SlowlogEntry struct, Store fields, methods)
- crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs (slowlog_cmd accepts Store, reads real data)
- crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs (redirect slowlog recording to store)

title: Implement metatables (getmetatable/setmetatable) for Lua 5.1 compatibility
description:
# Lua Metatables

## What
Metatables are a key Lua 5.1 feature. Some Redis Lua scripts use them for operator overloading or custom table behavior. Currently missing from the hand-written interpreter.

## Why
While rare in typical Redis scripts, metatables are part of the Lua 5.1 spec. Their absence could break third-party Lua libraries that depend on them.

## Implementation
1. Add a metatable field to LuaTable
2. Implement getmetatable()/setmetatable() global functions
3. Implement __index, __newindex, __tostring, __eq, __lt, __le, __add, __sub, __mul, __div, __mod, __pow, __concat, __len, __call metamethods
4. Dispatch to metamethods when operations fail on the base table

## Scope
This is significant work (~200-400 lines). Prioritize __index and __tostring as they are most commonly used.

## Files
- crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs
close_reason: Implemented getmetatable/setmetatable with real metatable storage (Option<LuaTable> in LuaTableInner). Added __index metamethod support via get_with_index() for table subscript, field access, and method calls. Supports chained __index (table→table). Added 5 tests covering get/set, __index fallback, chain, priority, and nil removal.

title: Wire CONFIG SET client-query-buffer-limit and proto-max-bulk-len to fr-server
description:
## Problem

fr-server/src/main.rs line 36 defines `const QUERY_BUFFER_LIMIT: usize = 1024 * 1024` (1 MiB). Redis defaults are:
- client-query-buffer-limit: 1 GiB
- proto-max-bulk-len: 512 MiB

The current 1 MiB limit is 1000x too restrictive. Any client sending a bulk string > 1 MiB (e.g., a large SET value) gets disconnected. This breaks real-world Redis usage patterns.

Additionally, fr-config/src/lib.rs line 66-67 has `CompatibilityGate` defaults of max_bulk_len=8MiB which also contradicts the config table's proto-max-bulk-len=512000000.

## What Redis Does

Both parameters are configurable via CONFIG SET. The query buffer limit controls the maximum size of the client input buffer (protecting against memory abuse). The proto-max-bulk-len controls the maximum size of a single bulk string in the RESP protocol.

## Implementation Plan

1. Add `query_buffer_limit` and `proto_max_bulk_len` to ServerState
2. Handle CONFIG SET for both parameters
3. In fr-server, read the limit from runtime instead of the const
4. Ensure fr-protocol's parse_frame respects the bulk length limit
5. Update CompatibilityGate defaults to match Redis (512 MiB for proto-max-bulk-len)

## Impact

This is critical for real-world usage — 1 MiB query buffer silently breaks any application storing values > 1 MiB, which is extremely common (images, serialized objects, large JSON documents).

## Files to Modify

- `crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs`: Add fields, CONFIG SET handler
- `crates/fr-server/src/main.rs`: Replace const with runtime value
- `crates/fr-config/src/lib.rs`: Fix CompatibilityGate defaults
close_reason: query_buffer_limit (1 GiB default) and proto_max_bulk_len (512 MiB default) now on ServerState, CONFIG SET/GET wired, fr-server reads runtime values

title: Fix RESP parser read_line off-by-one at MAX_LINE_LENGTH boundary
description:
## Problem

fr-protocol/src/lib.rs line 260: The read_line loop condition \"while i + 1 < max_search\" means the last position checked for \\r is max_search-2. If \\r falls at exactly max_search-1 (offset start + MAX_LINE_LENGTH - 1) with \\n at max_search, the valid \\r\\n pair is missed and the line is incorrectly rejected as LineTooLong.

## Impact

Low — this only affects RESP lines whose content (excluding \\r\\n) is exactly MAX_LINE_LENGTH-1 bytes long (64 KiB - 1). This is a rare edge case but violates the principle that any valid RESP frame should parse correctly.

## Fix

Change the loop condition from \"i + 1 < max_search\" to \"i + 1 <= max_search\" (or equivalently, \"i < max_search\"). Ensure the i+1 bounds check for \\n doesn't read past input.len().

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-protocol/src/lib.rs: read_line loop condition
close_reason: Fixed read_line loop to check full MAX_LINE_LENGTH range before declaring LineTooLong

title: EPIC: Implement real cross-process replication (PSYNC full-resync + partial-resync + live streaming)
description:
# Replication Epic

## Why This Is Critical
FEATURE_PARITY.md claims "parity_green" for replication, but this is false. The state machine logic (665 lines in fr-repl) is tested in isolation, but:
- REPLICAOF returns "OK" without actually connecting to a primary
- No bytes ever flow between two FrankenRedis processes
- Cannot replicate from a real Redis primary
- A real Redis replica cannot connect to FrankenRedis as primary
- Zero cross-process replication tests exist

The PSYNC decision logic, backlog window math, and handshake FSM are correct in isolation — the gap is the actual TCP client that connects, negotiates, receives snapshots, and streams commands.

## What Success Looks Like
1. `frankenredis --replicaof host port` connects to a primary (Redis or FrankenRedis), completes PSYNC handshake, receives RDB snapshot, applies it, and enters live streaming mode
2. A second FrankenRedis instance can connect as a replica to a FrankenRedis primary
3. Writes to the primary appear on the replica within bounded latency
4. Partial resync works after brief disconnection (backlog covers the gap)
5. An integration test demonstrates this end-to-end

## Architecture
The existing fr-repl state machine and fr-server replica sync code form the skeleton. The missing pieces are:
1. Real TCP client connection in REPLICAOF handler (currently a no-op)
2. Backlog buffer that actually stores propagated commands
3. Command propagation: after each write command, serialize and push to replica write buffers
4. Snapshot streaming: encode current store as RDB and send to new replicas
5. Live command streaming: send write commands to connected replicas in real-time

## Non-Goals for V1
- Multi-master / cluster replication
- Sentinel integration
- Diskless replication (can always go through RDB file)
close_reason: All sub-tasks complete. Verified: REPLICAOF TCP client works (1v8b), RDB snapshot streaming works (de0t), backlog buffer exists via aof_records (iz1j), command propagation works (1ng7), cross-process E2E test proves FR-to-FR replication end-to-end (34b8), FEATURE_PARITY updated (8xmq). Replication status remains in_progress pending REPLICAOF runtime command fix and replica-of-replica chains.

title: Add pub/sub cross-client integration test via TCP
description:
# Pub/Sub Cross-Client TCP Test

## What
An integration test that demonstrates pub/sub message delivery between multiple TCP clients connected to FrankenRedis.

## Why
Pub/sub delivery is tested via internal APIs (conformance fixtures) but never through actual independent TCP connections. A subscriber on one connection must receive messages published from a completely separate connection.

## Implementation

### Test 1: Basic Pub/Sub
1. Start frankenredis on random port
2. Client A (thread 1): SUBSCRIBE channel1, wait for subscription confirmation
3. Client B (thread 2): PUBLISH channel1 "hello" → verify returns 1 (one subscriber)
4. Client A: Read next message, verify ["message", "channel1", "hello"]
5. Client A: UNSUBSCRIBE channel1

### Test 2: Pattern Subscribe
1. Client A: PSUBSCRIBE "news.*"
2. Client B: PUBLISH news.sports "goal!" → returns 1
3. Client A: Verify ["pmessage", "news.*", "news.sports", "goal!"]

### Test 3: Multiple Subscribers
1. Client A: SUBSCRIBE channel1

title: Implement cjson.encode and cjson.decode in Lua evaluator
description:
## Problem

fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs line 1565: cjson is stubbed with a comment \"cjson stub (commonly used in Redis scripts)\". The cjson library is one of the most commonly used libraries in Redis Lua scripts for JSON serialization/deserialization.

## What Redis Provides

Redis bundles cjson (lua-cjson) which provides:
- cjson.encode(value) → JSON string
- cjson.decode(json_string) → Lua value
- cjson.encode_sparse_array(value) → JSON with sparse array handling

Common usage pattern:
  local data = cjson.decode(redis.call('GET', 'user:1'))
  data.visits = data.visits + 1
  redis.call('SET', 'user:1', cjson.encode(data))

## Implementation Plan

Since we already depend on serde_json in fr-conformance, we can add it to fr-command:

1. cjson.encode: Convert LuaValue → serde_json::Value → JSON string
   - LuaValue::Nil → JSON null
   - LuaValue::Bool → JSON bool
   - LuaValue::Number → JSON number
   - LuaValue::Str → JSON string (UTF-8)
   - LuaValue::Table → JSON object or array (based on table structure)

2. cjson.decode: Parse JSON string → serde_json::Value → LuaValue
   - JSON null → LuaValue::Nil
   - JSON bool → LuaValue::Bool
   - JSON number → LuaValue::Number
   - JSON string → LuaValue::Str
   - JSON array → LuaValue::Table (array part)
   - JSON object → LuaValue::Table (hash part)

## Edge Cases

- Empty table: encode as {} (object) or [] (array)? Redis cjson defaults to object.
- Mixed table (both array and hash keys): Redis cjson errors on this.
- Very large/deep structures: need recursion depth limit
- Non-UTF-8 strings: cjson.encode errors on these

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-command/Cargo.toml: Add serde_json dependency
- crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs: Replace stubs with real implementations
close_reason: cjson.encode/decode already fully implemented with custom JSON serializer. Fixed misleading 'stub' comment.

title: [EPIC] INFO and CLIENT LIST Accuracy
description:
## Overview

Several INFO sections and CLIENT LIST output contain hardcoded or missing dynamic values. While some were fixed (hz, maxclients, executable, version), several remain.

## Remaining Issues

1. CLIENT LIST addr field: Always reports 127.0.0.1:0 instead of actual client peer address. Fixing this requires tracking peer addresses per-client in the server's session management.

2. CLIENT LIST laddr field: Partially fixed (port now dynamic) but IP is still hardcoded to 127.0.0.1.

3. INFO replication repl_backlog_size: Hardcoded to 1048576 in fr-command. Should read from runtime's actual backlog size.

4. XINFO STREAM radix-tree metrics: Hardcoded to 1/0. These require internal stream-node accounting that doesn't exist yet. Lower priority since the radix-tree is a Redis implementation detail, not a user-facing metric.

## Architecture for Client Addresses

To fix CLIENT LIST properly:
1. When accept_connections creates a new ClientConnection, store the peer addr from TcpStream::peer_addr()
2. Add a peer_addr field to ClientSession
3. In CLIENT LIST/INFO, read the stored peer addr
4. This also enables CLIENT KILL by addr filtering

# Add keyspace_hits and keyspace_misses counters to Store

## What
Add two new pub u64 fields to Store: stat_keyspace_hits and stat_keyspace_misses.
Increment them in the appropriate access paths.

## Where to add counters
- stat_keyspace_hits: incremented when a key lookup succeeds (get, hget, etc.)
- stat_keyspace_misses: incremented when a key lookup returns None/not-found

## Key implementation points
- Add fields to Store struct (fr-store/src/lib.rs, near line 722)
- Initialize to 0 in Store::new()
- Increment in Store::get(), Store::exists(), and other read paths that check key existence
- The tricky part: many commands call internal store methods that don't distinguish hit vs miss at the Store level. The cleanest approach is to add an internal helper that records the hit/miss after any key-existence check.
- Do NOT add counters inside every individual command — add them at the Store method level where key lookups happen.

## Why this matters
- INFO stats reports keyspace_hits:0 and keyspace_misses:0 — monitoring tools use these for cache hit ratio calculations
- Redis OBJECT FREQ uses hit frequency; while we don't implement LFU, the counters enable future LFU eviction
- Conformance: redis-benchmark and redis-stat rely on these counters

- Conformance: INFO stats section should show non-zero keyspace_hits after operations

title: Implement lexical scoping / upvalue capture in Lua evaluator
description:
## Problem

fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs line 2274: When calling a LuaValue::Function, a completely fresh environment is created (Env::new()). This means closures cannot access variables from their defining scope.

Example that breaks:
  local x = 10
  local f = function() return x end
  return f()  -- returns nil, should return 10

This is a FUNDAMENTAL semantic bug that silently breaks any Lua code using closures, callbacks, or higher-order functions.

## How Real Lua Does It

Lua 5.1 uses upvalue capture: when a function is created, it captures references to local variables in enclosing scopes. These captured variables (upvalues) travel with the function closure.

## Implementation Options

### Option A: Environment Chain (simpler)
When creating a LuaValue::Function, snapshot the current environment as a parent chain. When the function is called, create a new env with the captured parent as the lookup fallback.

Data model change:
- LuaValue::Function currently stores (name, body_statements)
- Change to store (name, body_statements, captured_env: Option<Env>)
- In Env::get(), if local lookup fails, walk the captured_env chain

### Option B: Explicit Upvalue Tracking (more correct)
Track which variables are referenced by the function body. Only capture those specific bindings. More memory-efficient but harder to implement.

## Recommendation

Option A is much simpler and sufficient for Redis Lua scripts (which are typically not deeply nested). The performance cost is minimal since Env is just a HashMap.

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs: LuaValue::Function variant, Env struct, call_function, function definition handling

## Testing

- Test: local variable access from closure
- Test: nested closures (closure inside closure)
- Test: closure over loop variable
- Test: multiple closures sharing same upvalue
- Test: redis.call inside a closure
close_reason: Implemented upvalue capture via captured_env on LuaFunc. Functions now snapshot the lexical scope at definition time and restore it on call, enabling closures.

title: Add runtime-populated context fields to Store for INFO accuracy
description:
# Add runtime context fields to Store for cross-layer INFO reporting

## What
Add fields to Store that the runtime/server populates each tick so that fr-command's INFO handler can report accurate values for fields that originate in the runtime layer:
- pub stat_blocked_clients: u64 (number of currently blocked clients)
- pub stat_tracking_clients: u64 (number of tracking clients)
- pub maxmemory_bytes_live: usize (runtime's current maxmemory setting)

## Why a Store field instead of duplicating logic
The INFO command is dispatched through fr-command's dispatch_argv, which only has access to Store. The runtime has the real values but can't easily pass them through dispatch_argv's signature. The cleanest solution is for the server event loop to update these Store fields before each command batch, so INFO reads accurate values.

## Where to populate
- In fr-server/src/main.rs: before processing each client's commands, update store.stat_blocked_clients = blocked_tokens.len()
- In fr-runtime: when maxmemory changes, update store.maxmemory_bytes_live
- tracking_clients can stay 0 until CLIENT TRACKING is fully wired

## Key implementation points
- Add fields to Store struct with pub visibility
- Initialize to 0 in Store::new()
- Server event loop updates them each tick (cheap — just integer assignments)
- INFO handler in fr-command reads them instead of hardcoding 0

## Why this matters
- blocked_clients:0 is wrong when clients are blocked on BLPOP etc.
- maxmemory:0 in INFO is wrong when runtime has set maxmemory
- Monitoring tools use these for capacity planning

## Files to modify
- crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs (Store struct)
- crates/fr-server/src/main.rs (populate fields each tick)
- crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs (populate maxmemory_bytes_live on CONFIG SET)

title: Precompute ACL category->command mapping in OnceLock
description:
# Precompute ACL Category→Command Mapping in OnceLock

## What

command_categories() in crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs:62 and
commands_in_acl_category() in crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:8924 recompute
ACL category→command mappings from scratch on every call. Each call iterates
21 ACL categories × the entire COMMAND_TABLE (~200 commands) and does
split_whitespace().collect::<Vec<&str>>() on each command's flags string —
~4,200 Vec allocations per invocation.

After the April 9 short-circuit (frankenredis-zjii), the DEFAULT user (no
category rules) never hits this code path. But any user with ACL category
rules (e.g., +@read -@dangerous) still pays the full cost on every command.

## Why This Matters

Production Redis deployments commonly use ACL categories for least-privilege
security. A web app might configure: user webuser +@read +@write -@dangerous.
That user has non-empty allowed_categories, so the short-circuit doesn't apply,
and every command pays ~4,200 allocations for category resolution.

## Design

Use OnceLock<HashMap<&'static str, Vec<&'static str>>> to build the mapping
once at startup:

```rust
static ACL_CATEGORY_MAP: OnceLock<HashMap<&'static str, Vec<&'static str>>> = OnceLock::new();

fn commands_in_acl_category_cached(category: &str) -> &'static [&'static str] {
    let map = ACL_CATEGORY_MAP.get_or_init(|| {
        let mut m = HashMap::new();
        for &cat in ACL_CATEGORIES {
            m.insert(cat, commands_in_acl_category_uncached(cat));
        }
        m
    });
    map.get(category).map(|v| v.as_slice()).unwrap_or(&[])
}
```

Also precompute the reverse mapping (command→categories) so
command_categories() is a single HashMap lookup:

```rust
static COMMAND_CATEGORY_MAP: OnceLock<HashMap<&'static str, Vec<&'static str>>> = OnceLock::new();
```

## Acceptance Criteria

1. commands_in_acl_category uses OnceLock-cached mapping
2. command_categories uses OnceLock-cached reverse mapping
3. Zero per-command allocations for ACL category resolution
4. All ACL conformance tests pass
5. Users with non-trivial ACL rules see no throughput regression vs default user
6. Memory overhead is bounded (21 categories × 200 commands = ~4200 pointers = ~33KB)
close_reason: Cached ACL category mappings + command reverse map

title: Investigate severe throughput gap vs Redis baselines
description:
# Severe Performance Gap Against Redis Baseline

## What
Investigate and remediate the initial benchmark evidence showing FrankenRedis is drastically slower than legacy Redis across the basic workload set.

## Evidence
From `baselines/` captured on 2026-04-07:

- `SET`: FrankenRedis `1240.93 ops/sec` vs Redis `94402.47 ops/sec` (`1.31%` of Redis throughput)
- `GET`: FrankenRedis `1204.65 ops/sec` vs Redis `91142.35 ops/sec` (`1.32%` of Redis throughput)
- `MIXED`: FrankenRedis `1159.23 ops/sec` vs Redis `96834.08 ops/sec` (`1.20%` of Redis throughput)
- `INCR`: FrankenRedis `1176.59 ops/sec` vs Redis `95183.76 ops/sec` (`1.24%` of Redis throughput)
- `PIPELINE16`: FrankenRedis `1221.37 ops/sec` vs Redis `860900.42 ops/sec` (`0.14%` of Redis throughput)

Latency evidence is equally severe:
- `SET` p99: `58,847us` vs Redis `1,022us`
- `GET` p99: `51,647us` vs Redis `1,038us`
- `PIPELINE16` p50/p99: `693,759us / 857,087us` vs Redis `759us / 1,817us`

## Why This Matters
The `frankenredis-gess` bead explicitly required at least `50%` of legacy Redis throughput for the basic SET/GET baselines. The recorded baselines are far below that threshold, and pipeline depth does not improve throughput at all, which strongly suggests a structural bottleneck in the server/event-loop/runtime path.

## Investigation Targets
1. Profile FrankenRedis under the `set`, `get`, and `pipeline16` workloads.
2. Determine whether the dominant bottleneck is in:
   - `fr-server` event loop / socket IO
   - `fr-runtime::execute_frame` dispatch path
   - RESP parsing / encoding overhead
   - store keyspace representation / DB namespacing
   - per-command bookkeeping (`INFO` stats, slowlog, latency tracker, evidence logging)
